---
title: "Boundary Conditions in Practice"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
    fig_caption: yes
    dpi: 200
    dev.args: list(pointsize=11)
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Boundary conditions in practice}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
---

# Introduction

This article turns the mathematical boundary statements used throughout the theory pages into practical consequences for model choice and interpretation.

# Why boundary choice matters

Boundary condition is one of the main reasons that two geometrically similar targets can require very different models. The same sphere may be treated as rigid, pressure-release, fluid-filled, elastic-shelled, or calibration-like depending on the physical problem.

# Practical mapping

As a first approximation:

- rigid or pressure-release canonical bodies point toward `SPHMS`, `PSMS`, or `FCMS`,
- fluid-filled canonical bodies point toward the same modal families with fluid continuity conditions,
- elastic shells point toward `ESSMS`,
- weak fluid-like bodies point toward `DWBA`, `SDWBA`, or `HPA`,
- high-frequency fluid-interface approximations point toward `TRCM` or the body term of `KRM`.

# Common failure mode

One of the most common mistakes is to select a geometry-matched model while silently mismatching the boundary regime. That usually produces a mathematically valid answer to the wrong physical question.

# Related reading

- [Choosing a model](../model-selection/model-selection.html)
- [Material properties and acoustic utilities](../material-properties/material-properties.html)
- all model-specific theory pages.
